parts.content: Using your answer to part 1, or otherwise, find the area of the parallelogram with two sides $\overrightarrow{OP}$ and $\overrightarrow{OQ}$
parts.markscheme:
- State the relationship for area: Area $= |\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q}|$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Substitute the components into the magnitude formula: $\sqrt{(-7)^{2}+7^{2}+7^{2}}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Calculate the final answer: $7 \sqrt{3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Area Formula
steps.content:
To find the area of a parallelogram defined by two vectors $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{q}$ starting from the same origin $O$, we use a key geometric property of the vector product. The area is equal to the magnitude of the cross product of the two vectors:

$$\text{Area} = |\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q}|$$

This is a standard application of vectors in Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry.

steps.title: Recall the Cross Product
steps.content:
From the first part of the question, we found the cross product of $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{q}$:

$$\mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{q} = -7\mathbf{i} + 7\mathbf{j} + 7\mathbf{k}$$

If you haven't calculated this yet, you would set up a determinant using the components of $\mathbf{p} = (3, 2, 1)$ and $\mathbf{q} = (1, 3, -2)$.

steps.title: Apply Magnitude Formula
steps.content:
Now, we need to find the magnitude (length) of this resulting vector. We use the magnitude formula provided in the **Vectors** section of your data booklet:

$$|\mathbf{v}| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + v_3^2}$$

Substituting our components $(-7, 7, 7)$ into this formula gives:

$$\text{Area} = \sqrt{(-7)^2 + 7^2 + 7^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate and Simplify
steps.content:
Let's perform the arithmetic:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= \sqrt{49 + 49 + 49} \\ &= \sqrt{147} \end{aligned}$$

To simplify the radical, notice that $147 = 49 \times 3$. Since $\sqrt{49} = 7$, we get:

$$\text{Area} = 7\sqrt{3}$$

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, find the 'norm' or 'magnitude' function. For example, on a Casio, use Norm([-7, 7, 7]).

ti84.instructions: Go to the Home screen. Enter √( (-7)² + 7² + 7² ) and press ENTER. To get the exact radical form, simplify 147 manually or use a radical simplifier tool if available.

desmos.instructions: Define a vector v = (-7, 7, 7) and then calculate length(v) or distance((0,0,0), (-7, 7, 7)).

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the vector (-7, 7, 7).

steps.title: Final Answer State
steps.content:
The area of the parallelogram formed by the vectors $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{q}$ is:

$$\text{Area} = 7\sqrt{3} \approx 12.1$$

steps.title: Identify the approach
steps.content:
To find the area of a parallelogram using the dot product and trigonometry, we recall that a parallelogram can be split into two identical triangles. The area of a triangle formed by vectors $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{q}$ is $\frac{1}{2}|\mathbf{p}||\mathbf{q}|\sin\theta$. Therefore, the area of the parallelogram is simply:

$$\text{Area} = |\mathbf{p}||\mathbf{q}|\sin\theta$$

To find $\sin\theta$, we will first use the scalar (dot) product to find $\cos\theta$.

steps.title: Calculate magnitudes
steps.content:
First, we need the magnitudes of the position vectors $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{q}$. We use the magnitude formula from the data booklet:

$$|\mathbf{v}|=\sqrt{v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}+v_{3}^{2}}$$

For $\mathbf{p}=3\mathbf{i}+2\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$:
$$|\mathbf{p}| = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{14}$$

For $\mathbf{q}=\mathbf{i}+3\mathbf{j}-2\mathbf{k}$:
$$|\mathbf{q}| = \sqrt{1^2 + 3^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{14}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the square root function to calculate sqrt(14).

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(14) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 14 to verify magnitude values.

steps.title: Find the dot product
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the dot product $\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{q}$ by multiplying the corresponding components and summing them:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{q} &= (3)(1) + (2)(3) + (1)(-2) \\ &= 3 + 6 - 2 \\ &= 7 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine cosine of theta
steps.content:
Now we use the scalar product formula from the data booklet to find $\cos\theta$:

$$\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{w}=|\mathbf{v}||\mathbf{w}|\cos\theta$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} 7 &= \sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{14} \cdot \cos\theta \\ 7 &= 14 \cos\theta \\ \cos\theta &= \frac{7}{14} = \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find sine of theta
steps.content:
To calculate the area, we need $\sin\theta$. We use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet:

$$\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \frac{1}{4} + \sin^2\theta &= 1 \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{3}{4} \\ \sin\theta &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

(Note: $\sin\theta$ is positive because the angle between two vectors is between $0$ and $\pi$).

steps.title: Calculate the final area
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the magnitudes and $\sin\theta$ into the area formula for the parallelogram:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= |\mathbf{p}||\mathbf{q}|\sin\theta \\ &= \sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{14} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ &= 14 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ &= 7\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

The area of the parallelogram is $7\sqrt{3}$ square units.

parts.content: Find a vector equation of the line of intersection of the planes $\Pi_1$ and $\Pi_2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the direction vector of the line of intersection:
  $$\vec{d} = \vec{n}_1 \times \vec{n}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ -7 \\ -7 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>M1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

- Find a point on the line of intersection by setting $z = 0$:
  From $\Pi_1$, $x + 2y = 4 \implies x = 4 - 2y$

- Substitute $x$ into the equation for $\Pi_2$:
  $$3(4 - 2y) - y = 10 \implies 12 - 7y = 10 \implies y = \frac{2}{7}$$ 

- Calculate the corresponding $x$-coordinate:
  $$x = 4 - 2\left(\frac{2}{7}\right) = \frac{24}{7}$$

- State the position vector of the point:
  $$\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{24}{7} \\ \frac{2}{7} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Write the vector equation of the line:
  $$\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{24}{7} \\ \frac{2}{7} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ -7 \\ -7 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Verify that the line of intersection found is perpendicular to the normal vectors of both planes.
parts.markscheme:
- State the normal vectors:
  $$\vec{n}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \vec{n}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

- Compute the dot product of $\vec{d}$ and $\vec{n}_1$:
  $$\vec{d} \cdot \vec{n}_1 = (7)(1) + (-7)(2) + (-7)(-1) = 7 - 14 + 7 = 0$$ <marks>A1</marks>
  This shows that $\vec{d}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{n}_1$.

- Compute the dot product of $\vec{d}$ and $\vec{n}_2$:
  $$\vec{d} \cdot \vec{n}_2 = (7)(3) + (-7)(-1) + (-7)(4) = 21 + 7 - 28 = 0$$ <marks>A1</marks>
  This shows that $\vec{d}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{n}_2$.

- Conclude that the direction vector $\vec{d}$ is perpendicular to the normal vectors of both planes. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the angle $\theta$ between two vectors $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{w}$, we use the scalar (dot) product formula provided in the IB Data Booklet. 

$$\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w} = |\mathbf{v}||\mathbf{w}| \cos \theta$$

Rearranging this to solve for $\cos \theta$ gives us:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w}}{|\mathbf{v}||\mathbf{w}|}$$

steps.title: Calculate the angle theta
steps.content:
Based on the dot product and magnitudes calculated in the previous parts of the question, we have the ratio $\frac{3}{13}$. We substitute this into our formula and solve for $\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta &= \frac{3}{13} \\ \theta &= \arccos\left(\frac{3}{13}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find the value in degrees:

$$\theta \approx 76.7^\circ$$

steps.title: Compare with safe range
steps.content:
The question asks if this angle falls within the safe range of $30^\circ$ to $60^\circ$. 

Comparing our calculated angle:
$$76.7^\circ > 60^\circ$$

Because $76.7^\circ$ is outside the interval $[30, 60]$, the angle is **not within the safe range**.

steps.title: Visualize the vector triangle
steps.content:
To use the **Geometric Cosine Rule**, we treat the two force vectors as two sides of a triangle, let's call them $a$ and $b$. The difference between these vectors forms the third side, $c$. The angle $\theta$ between the two original vectors is the angle opposite side $c$.

This approach allows us to use standard trigonometry to find the angle without needing the dot product formula directly.

steps.title: State the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
From the **Geometry and Trigonometry** section of your data booklet, we use the formula for the side of a triangle:

$$c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab\cos C$$

In our case, $C$ is the angle $\theta$ we are looking for. We can rearrange this formula to solve for $\cos \theta$:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab}$$

steps.title: Calculate the angle theta
steps.content:
Using the side lengths derived from the vector magnitudes (provided in previous parts or calculations), the markscheme indicates that the ratio simplifies to:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{3}{13}$$

To find $\theta$, we take the inverse cosine:

$$\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{3}{13}\right)$$

Let's calculate this value in degrees.

Using the calculator, we find:

$$\theta \approx 76.7^\circ$$

This calculation earned the first two marks (<marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>). Now we need to determine if this fits the safe range.

steps.title: Compare with safe range

The safe range for the angle is given as $30^\circ$ to $60^\circ$. 

Since our calculated angle $76.7^\circ$ is greater than $60^\circ$, the angle is **not within the safe range**.

This final reasoning step earns the last mark (<marks>R1</marks>).

Rearranging for $\theta$, we get:

parts.content: Find the angle between the two planes.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify normal vectors from plane equations:
$\vec{n_1} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\3\\-1\end{pmatrix}$ and $\vec{n_2} = \begin{pmatrix}-1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}$

- Attempt to use the dot product formula:
$\cos \theta = \frac{\vec{n_1} \cdot \vec{n_2}}{|\vec{n_1}||\vec{n_2}|}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate dot product and magnitudes:
$\vec{n_1} \cdot \vec{n_2} = 2(-1) + 3(2) + (-1)(2) = 2$
$|\vec{n_1}| = \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{14}$
$|\vec{n_2}| = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 2^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate final answer:
$\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{2}{3\sqrt{14}}\right) \approx 79.7^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Find a point that lies on both planes.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiply Equation (1) by 2:
$4x + 6y - 2z = 8 \quad (1')$

- Add Equation (2) and (1') to eliminate $z$:
$-x + 2y + 2z + 4x + 6y - 2z = 6 + 8$
$3x + 8y = 14 \quad (3)$

- Let $x = 0$ and solve for $y$:
Substitute $x = 0$ into $(3)$:
$8y = 14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{7}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $x = 0$ and $y = \frac{7}{4}$ into $\Pi_2$:
$-0 + 2\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) + 2z = 6$
$2z = 6 - \frac{7}{2} = \frac{5}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = \frac{5}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Verify the point satisfies both original equations:
For $\Pi_1$: $2(0) + 3\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) - \frac{5}{4} = \frac{21-5}{4} = 4$
For $\Pi_2$: $-0 + 2\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) + 2\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) = \frac{14+10}{4} = 6$
Both equations are satisfied. <marks>A1</marks>

- State final answer:
The point $\left(0, \frac{7}{4}, \frac{5}{4}\right)$ lies on both planes.

3 marks total

steps.title: Strategy: Fixing a variable
steps.content:
Since we need to find *a* point that lies on both planes, and the intersection of two non-parallel planes is a line (containing infinite points), we can choose an arbitrary value for one variable to find a specific point on that line.

We will use the **Fixing One Variable** method by setting $x = 0$. This simplifies our 3-variable system into a 2-variable system.

steps.title: Substitute x = 0
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 0$ into the equations for $\Pi_1$ and $\Pi_2$:

For $\Pi_1$: 
$$2(0) + 3y - z = 4 \Rightarrow 3y - z = 4$$

For $\Pi_2$:
$$-0 + 2y + 2z = 6 \Rightarrow 2y + 2z = 6$$

steps.title: Solve for y and z
steps.content:
Now we have a system of two simultaneous equations:

1) $3y - z = 4$
2) $2y + 2z = 6$

From equation (1), we can express $z$ in terms of $y$:
$$z = 3y - 4$$

Substitute this into equation (2):
$$2y + 2(3y - 4) = 6$$
$$2y + 6y - 8 = 6$$
$$8y = 14$$
$$y = \frac{14}{8} = \frac{7}{4}$$

steps.title: Calculate z
steps.content:
Now substitute $y = \frac{7}{4}$ back into our expression for $z$:

$$z = 3\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) - 4$$
$$z = \frac{21}{4} - \frac{16}{4}$$
$$z = \frac{5}{4}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
We have found the coordinates $x=0$, $y=\frac{7}{4}$, and $z=\frac{5}{4}$.

The point lying on both planes is:
$$\left(0, \frac{7}{4}, \frac{5}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Verify the solution
steps.content:
It is good practice to check if this point satisfies both original equations.

For $\Pi_1$:
$$2(0) + 3\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) - \frac{5}{4} = \frac{21}{4} - \frac{5}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \quad \text{(Correct)}$$

For $\Pi_2$:
$$-0 + 2\left(\frac{7}{4}\right) + 2\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) = \frac{14}{4} + \frac{10}{4} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \quad \text{(Correct)}$$

steps.title: Set up the system of equations
steps.content:
First, we identify the system of linear equations from the two plane equations given in the question. We align the variables $x, y, z$ and the constants:

$$\begin{cases} 2x + 3y - z = 4 \\ -x + 2y + 2z = 6 \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Construct the augmented matrix
steps.content:
We extract the coefficients and constants to form a $2 \times 4$ augmented matrix. The columns represent coefficients for $x$, $y$, $z$, and the constant term respectively.

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & -1 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 & 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

steps.title: Calculate RREF using GDC
steps.content:
Now, enter this matrix into your graphing calculator and use the **rref** (reduced row echelon form) command. This will solve the system for the leading variables ($x$ and $y$) in terms of the free variable ($z$).

Converting the decimal output to fractions (using **>Frac** on your calculator) makes the solution easier to work with.

2. Enter the matrix [[2, 3, -1, 4], [-1, 2, 2, 6]]
3. Select OPTN > MAT > Rref
4. Execute Rref(Mat A)

steps.title: Interpret the RREF output
steps.content:
The calculator output in fractional form is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -8/7 & -10/7 \\ 0 & 1 & 3/7 & 16/7 \end{bmatrix}$$

This corresponds to the equations:

$$x - \frac{8}{7}z = -\frac{10}{7}$$
$$y + \frac{3}{7}z = \frac{16}{7}$$

steps.title: Determine a specific point
steps.content:
Since we have two equations with three variables, there are infinite solutions (a line of intersection). To find **a point**, we can choose any convenient value for $z$.

Let $z = 0$. Substituting this into our equations:

$$x = -\frac{10}{7} + \frac{8}{7}(0) = -\frac{10}{7}$$
$$y = \frac{16}{7} - \frac{3}{7}(0) = \frac{16}{7}$$

So, a point on both planes is:
$$\left(-\frac{10}{7}, \frac{16}{7}, 0\right)$$

Alternatively, to match the markscheme which sets $x=0$, we could set $x=0$ in the first equation:
$$0 - \frac{8}{7}z = -\frac{10}{7}$$
$$z = \frac{5}{4}$$

calculator.description: Verify the point (-10/7, 16/7, 0) satisfies Plane 1: 2x + 3y - z = 4

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Any valid point is acceptable. Using our first result:

The point is:
$$\left(-\frac{10}{7}, \frac{16}{7}, 0\right)$$

parts.content: Find a direction vector of the line of intersection of these planes.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize direction vector is the cross product of normal vectors:
$\vec{d} = \vec{n}_1 \times \vec{n}_2$

- Set up the determinant to compute the cross product:
$\vec{d} = \begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ 2 & 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & 2 \end{vmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Expand the determinant:
$\vec{d} = \begin{pmatrix} 3(2) - (-1)(2) \\ (-1)(-1) - 2(2) \\ 2(2) - 3(-1) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 - (-2) \\ 1 - 4 \\ 4 - (-3) \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State final answer:
$\vec{d} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ -3 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Find the point of intersection of the line $L_1$ and the plane $\pi$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the line components into the plane equation: $2(1 + 4\lambda) - (2 - \lambda) + (3 + 2\lambda) = 5$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify the equation: $3 + 11\lambda = 5 \Rightarrow 11\lambda = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solve for $\lambda$: $\lambda = \frac{2}{11}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $\lambda$ to find the point of intersection: $\mathbf{P} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + \frac{2}{11}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{19}{11} \\ \frac{20}{11} \\ \frac{37}{11} \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Accept decimal approximation $\mathbf{P} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 1.73 \\ 1.82 \\ 3.36 \end{pmatrix}$</note>

steps.title: Define parametric equations
steps.content:
To find where the line and plane meet, we first express the general point on the line $L_1$ in terms of the parameter $\lambda$. From the given vector equation, we can write expressions for $x$, $y$, and $z$:

$$\begin{cases} x = 1 + 4\lambda \\ y = 2 - \lambda \\ z = 3 + 2\lambda \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Substitute into plane equation
steps.content:
The point of intersection must satisfy the equation of the plane $\pi$. We substitute our parametric expressions for $x$, $y$, and $z$ into the Cartesian equation $2x - y + z = 5$:

$$2(1 + 4\lambda) - (2 - \lambda) + (3 + 2\lambda) = 5$$

This step is crucial for earning your first method mark (**M1**) in the exam.

highlights.text: plane $\pi$ given by the equation $2x - y + z = 5$

steps.title: Solve for the parameter
steps.content:
Now, we simplify the equation to find the value of $\lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned} (2 + 8\lambda) - 2 + \lambda + 3 + 2\lambda &= 5 \\ 3 + 11\lambda &= 5 \\ 11\lambda &= 2 \\ \lambda &= \frac{2}{11} \end{aligned}$$

Solving this correctly earns two accuracy marks (**A1A1**): one for the simplified equation and one for the value of $\lambda$.

gdc.instructions: In the Equation/Solver menu, enter '2(1 + 4X) - (2 - X) + (3 + 2X) = 5' and solve for X.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] [ALPHA] [B] (Numeric Solver). Enter '0 = 2(1 + 4X) - (2 - X) + (3 + 2X) - 5'. Highlight 'X=' and press [ALPHA] [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the input bar and view the intersection with the x-axis to find the value of x (which represents $\lambda$).

calculator.description: You can use your GDC to solve for $\lambda$ by graphing or using the numerical solver.

steps.title: Calculate the intersection point
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute $\lambda = \frac{2}{11}$ back into our parametric equations from Step 0 to find the coordinates of the point $P$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 1 + 4\left(\frac{2}{11}\right) = \frac{11}{11} + \frac{8}{11} = \frac{19}{11} \\ y &= 2 - \frac{2}{11} = \frac{22}{11} - \frac{2}{11} = \frac{20}{11} \\ z &= 3 + 2\left(\frac{2}{11}\right) = \frac{33}{11} + \frac{4}{11} = \frac{37}{11} \end{aligned}$$

So, the point of intersection is $\left( \frac{19}{11}, \frac{20}{11}, \frac{37}{11} \right)$. This final answer earns the last accuracy mark (**A1**).

ti84.instructions: Calculate each coordinate individually: '1 + 4(2/11)', '2 - (2/11)', and '3 + 2(2/11)'. Use [MATH] [1] (>Frac) to convert to fractions.

calculator.description: Perform vector addition to find the final coordinates.

steps.title: Express line in parametric form
steps.content:
To find where the line and plane intersect, we first express the position of any point on line $L_1$ in terms of the parameter $\lambda$. From the vector equation, we can write the individual $x$, $y$, and $z$ components:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 1 + 4\lambda \\ y &= 2 - \lambda \\ z &= 3 + 2\lambda \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert to Cartesian form
steps.content:
We can eliminate $\lambda$ by solving for it in each equation. This gives us the Cartesian form of the line:

$$\lambda = \frac{x-1}{4} = \frac{y-2}{-1} = \frac{z-3}{2}$$

By taking pairs of these ratios, we can generate two distinct linear equations:
1. $\frac{x-1}{4} = \frac{y-2}{-1} \Rightarrow -(x-1) = 4(y-2) \Rightarrow x + 4y = 9$
2. $\frac{y-2}{-1} = \frac{z-3}{2} \Rightarrow 2(y-2) = -(z-3) \Rightarrow 2y + z = 7$

steps.title: Set up the 3x3 system
steps.content:
We now have a system of three linear equations using the two equations from the line and the one equation for the plane $\pi$:

$$\begin{cases} x + 4y = 9 \\ 2y + z = 7 \\ 2x - y + z = 5 \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
We can solve this system using substitution. From the first two equations, we can express $x$ and $z$ in terms of $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 9 - 4y \\ z &= 7 - 2y \end{aligned}$$

Substitute these into the plane's equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 2(9 - 4y) - y + (7 - 2y) &= 5 \\ 18 - 8y - y + 7 - 2y &= 5 \\ 25 - 11y &= 5 \\ -11y &= -20 \\ y &= \frac{20}{11} \end{aligned}$$

Now, calculate $x$ and $z$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 9 - 4\left(\frac{20}{11}\right) = \frac{99-80}{11} = \frac{19}{11} \\ z &= 7 - 2\left(\frac{20}{11}\right) = \frac{77-40}{11} = \frac{37}{11} \end{aligned}$$

5. Select your matrix and press [ENTER].

In decimal form, this is approximately $(1.73, 1.82, 3.36)$. This matches the result found in the markscheme by substituting the parameter directly.

=(-9-(-2))\mathbf{i}-(6-1)\mathbf{j}+(-4-(-3))\mathbf{k}=(-7,-5,-1).
\] <marks>M1</marks>
- Determine the Cartesian equation using \(\mathbf{n}=(-7,-5,-1)\) and point \(A(1,2,3)\):
\[-7(x-1)-5(y-2)-1(z-3)=0 \Rightarrow -7x-5y-z+20=0.\]
- State the simplified equation: \(7x+5y+z=20\). <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Verify that point \(D(3,1,1)\) does not lie on \(\Pi\), and find the perpendicular distance from \(D\) to \(\Pi\).
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the coordinates of \(D\) into the plane equation:
\[
7(3)+5(1)+1=21+5+1=27 \neq 20.
\]
Since \(27 \neq 20\), \(D\) does not lie on \(\Pi\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Apply the distance formula:
\[
\text{Distance} = \frac{|7(3)+5(1)+1-20|}{\sqrt{7^2+5^2+1^2}} = \frac{|27-20|}{\sqrt{49+25+1}}.
\] <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final distance:
\[
\frac{7}{\sqrt{75}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{7}{5\sqrt{3}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{7\sqrt{3}}{15}.
\] <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
Find the acute angle between the plane \(\Pi\) and the line
\[
L:\ \mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-3\\-2\end{pmatrix}.
\]
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the direction vector of \(L\), \(\mathbf{d}=(1,-3,-2)\), and the normal to \(\Pi\), \(\mathbf{n}=(7,5,1)\). <marks>A1</marks>
- State the formula for the angle \(\theta\) between the line and plane:
\[
\sin\theta=\frac{|\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{n}|}{\|\mathbf{d}\|\, \|\mathbf{n}\|}.
\] <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute the values:
\[
\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{n}=1(7)+(-3)(5)+(-2)(1)=7-15-2=-10.
\]
\[
\|\mathbf{d}\|=\sqrt{1^2+(-3)^2+(-2)^2}=\sqrt{14},\ \|\mathbf{n}\|=\sqrt{7^2+5^2+1^2}=\sqrt{75}=5\sqrt{3}.
\] <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the angle:
\[
\sin\theta=\frac{|-10|}{\sqrt{14}\cdot 5\sqrt{3}}=\frac{10}{5\sqrt{42}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{42}} \Rightarrow \theta=\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{42}}\right).
\] <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

Substitute \(y=0\) into \(7x+5y+z=20\) to get \(7x+z=20\).
Let \(x=t\), then \(z=20-7t\). The direction vector is \(\mathbf{l}=(1,0,-7)\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate a vector \(\mathbf{v}\) in \(\Pi\) perpendicular to \(\mathbf{l}\) (using \(\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{n}\times\mathbf{l}\)):
\[
\mathbf{v}=\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf{i}&\mathbf{j}&\mathbf{k}\\
7&5&1\\
1&0&-7
\end{vmatrix}
=(-35-0)\mathbf{i}-(-49-1)\mathbf{j}+(0-5)\mathbf{k}=(-35,50,-5).
\] <marks>M1</marks>

Using the parallel vector \((7,-10,1)\):

\] <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the shortest length $|HX|$.
parts.markscheme:
- Find $\overrightarrow{EC}$:
  $\overrightarrow{EC} = \mathbf{c} - \mathbf{e} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Express $\overrightarrow{EX}$ in terms of $\lambda$:
  $\overrightarrow{EX} = \lambda \overrightarrow{EC} = \begin{pmatrix} 5\lambda \\ 2\lambda \\ -\lambda \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Find expression for $\overrightarrow{HX}$ (using $\overrightarrow{HE} = \overrightarrow{DA}$):
  $\overrightarrow{HX} = \overrightarrow{HE} + \overrightarrow{EX} = (\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{d}) + \overrightarrow{EX}$
  $\overrightarrow{HX} = \begin{pmatrix} -5 - (-1) \\ 2 - 1 \\ -4 - 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 5\lambda \\ 2\lambda \\ -\lambda \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -4+5\lambda \\ 1+2\lambda \\ -4-\lambda \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- State condition for shortest length (perpendicularity):
  $\overrightarrow{HX} \cdot \overrightarrow{EC} = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute and expand the dot product:
  $5(-4+5\lambda) + 2(1+2\lambda) - 1(-4-\lambda) = 0$ <marks>M1 M1</marks>

- Solve for $\lambda$:
  $-20 + 25\lambda + 2 + 4\lambda + 4 + \lambda = 0$
  $30\lambda - 14 = 0 \implies \lambda = \frac{14}{30} = \frac{7}{15}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate magnitude $|\overrightarrow{HX}|$:
  $|\overrightarrow{HX}| = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{5}{3}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{29}{15}\right)^{2} + \left(-\frac{67}{15}\right)^{2}}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>
  $= \sqrt{\frac{397}{15}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Final answer:
  Shortest length is $5.14$ units <marks>A1</marks>

14 marks total

- Square both sides: $|\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}|^{2} = |\mathbf{a}|^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute identity $\sin ^{2} \theta = 1-\cos ^{2} \theta$: $|\mathbf{a}|^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2}\left(1-\cos ^{2} \theta\right)$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Expand the expression: $|\mathbf{a}|^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2}-|\mathbf{a}|^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Group terms: $|\mathbf{a}|^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2}-(|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}| \cos \theta)^{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute scalar product definition $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = |\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}| \cos \theta$: $|\mathbf{a}|^{2}|\mathbf{b}|^{2}-(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b})^{2}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Conclude the proof: Hence shown.

parts.content: Hence or otherwise, find the area of triangle $ABC$.

- State final area: $\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find $a \times b$
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the cross product of vectors $a$ and $b$:

  $$a \times b = \begin{pmatrix} -6 \\ -3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

parts.content: Show that $a \times b$ is perpendicular to both $a$ and $b$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the dot product of $(a \times b)$ and $a$:
  $$(a \times b) \cdot a = -12 + 0 + 12 = 0$$
  <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Calculate the dot product of $(a \times b)$ and $b$:
  $$(a \times b) \cdot b = -6 - 6 + 12 = 0$$
  <marks>M1 A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the vector product $\overrightarrow{DA} \times \overrightarrow{DC}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to find vectors $\overrightarrow{DA}$ and $\overrightarrow{DC}$: $\overrightarrow{DA} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 4 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\overrightarrow{DC} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Attempt to calculate the cross product: $\begin{vmatrix} \mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ -2 & 4 & -3 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct expansion: $\mathbf{i}(4 - (-9)) - \mathbf{j}(-2 - (-3)) + \mathbf{k}(-6 - 4)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State result: $\begin{pmatrix} 13 \\ -1 \\ -10 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Hence, calculate the volume of the parallelepiped.
parts.markscheme:
- Method to find volume using scalar triple product or $|\overrightarrow{DA} \times \overrightarrow{DC}| \times \text{height}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Finding vector $\overrightarrow{DH}$: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitution into volume formula: $|(\overrightarrow{DA} \times \overrightarrow{DC}) \cdot \overrightarrow{DH}| = |13(1) + (-1)(1) + (-10)(5)|$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate final volume: $|-38| = 38$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total
